topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the familiar spine of "Whispers in the Gloom," a book he’d cherished since he was a boy. Now, the pages felt like a battlefield. He’d spent the last three days poring over the latest collection, the prose feeling strangely hollow, echoing familiar structures he’d painstakingly crafted in his own notebooks. A cold knot tightened in his stomach every time he encountered a passage that mirrored his own unique metaphors—metaphors he’d poured sleepless nights into developing. He kept running a hand through his already disheveled hair, the movement jerky. When he finally found the damning evidence—a direct, almost identical sequence of events described in the author’s new novella—a sudden, sharp intake of breath escaped him. His hands began to tremble, the paper slipping slightly in his grip. He needed air. He needed distance.

[story 2]

“It’s impossible,” Clara whispered, the words catching in her throat like shards of glass. She stared at the manuscript on her desk, the damning comparison glaring back at her. Every line felt like a betrayal, a violation of the careful, meticulous world she had built around her creative process. Her jaw was clenched so tightly her temples throbbed. She stood up abruptly, pacing the narrow confines of her studio, her heels clicking a frantic rhythm against the wooden floor. She picked up her phone, her thumb hovering over the contact for the author, but stopped. The impulse to confront was warring with a deep, nauseating sense of disorientation. She felt a clammy sweat prickle her hairline, and she forced herself to sit down, trying to steady the rapid pulse hammering against her ribs.

[story 3]

Marcus kept re-reading the passages, his eyes darting back and forth across the text, searching for any deviation, any hint of originality that wasn't his own. The silence of his apartment was oppressive, amplifying the sound of his own shallow breathing. He pressed the heels of his palms against his eyes, trying to physically block out the visual onslaught. A deep, unsettling churning started in his gut, a physical reaction to the sheer audacity of the theft. He slammed the book shut, the sound echoing unnervingly loud in the quiet room. He stood up, walking to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, his posture rigid, every muscle coiled tight beneath his worn sweater. He didn't know what to do next, only that the air felt too thin to draw in.
